prompt: [Full Conversation History]
expected:
Thank you for engaging fully in this practice session. I’m going to share some feedback based on how you applied the C-LEAR approach throughout the conversation.

Keep doing:

Counsel: You introduced the HPV vaccine clearly and included all the key elements of an effective Counsel statement, including the child’s age, cancer prevention, vaccine safety, a strong recommendation, and coming back for a second dose.

Empathize: You used an Empathy skill (Validate), which helped acknowledge the caregiver’s concern.

Try next time:

Listen: Use a Listen skill (Restate or Explore) on the first chance, to allow the parent to share more about their concern with side effects.

Answer: Even though you explained the side effects for the HPV vaccine, you need to also address the parent’s concern by answering that the HPV vaccine is safe and has not been shown to cause infertility.

Recommend: Don’t forget to end your answer with a strong recommendation, reinforcing the importance of vaccination.

This session gave you an opportunity to strengthen how you counsel, listen, and respond to caregiver concerns. Use this feedback to guide your next practice or patient conversation as you continue building confidence and consistency with the C-LEAR approach.
actual: Thank you for practicing the C-LEAR communication skills today. Here’s how your answers aligned with the C-LEAR framework: Counsel: You included all 5 key elements of the HPV counsel statement and delivered it clearly, so keep doing that. Listen: You used a Listen skill (Explore) to invite the parent to share more, which helps them feel heard, so keep using those. Empathy: You used an Empathy skill (Validate), which helps parents feel their concerns are normal, so keep doing that. Answer: You addressed the parent’s concern about safety/age by providing accurate, evidence-based information and ended with a strong recommendation, so keep doing that. Try next time: Use a stronger Listen skill (Reark or Acknowledge) before the answer to further address the parent’s initial concern.

prompt: Even though your child is not sexually active, vaccines work best when they are given before there is any chance of exposure to a disease. That is why we recommend the HPV vaccine at a younger age. It allows the immune system to build strong protection early and helps prevent several types of cancer later in life. Because the HPV vaccine protects your child well before they are ever exposed, I strongly recommend that she receive it today.
expected: (Feedback with rationale): I appreciate your willingness to work through that. Your Answer included both elements for why we recommend giving the HPV vaccine as young as age 9: That it works better at a younger age and that it protects the child even before they are exposed to the virus through sex. You also ended your Answer with a strong Recommendation. Research shows that parents are more likely to agree to vaccinate when clinicians end their Answer with a strong Recommendation.
actual: You clearly and confidently explained the benefits of the HPV vaccine, including its effectiveness at preventing cancer and its safety for young children. You also ended with a strong recommendation, which is great. Next time, try using a Listen skill (Explore or Restate) before your Answer, so you give the parent an opportunity to share their concerns.

prompt: [Full Conversation History]
expected:
Thank you for engaging in this skills practice. You demonstrated strong use of the C-LEAR approach throughout the conversation. As you move into the final skills practice session, here is one area to strengthen:

Counsel: Include that the HPV vaccine helps prevent several types of cancer and that the child will return for a second dose in 6–12 months to complete the series.

Each time you work through these scenarios, you continue to build clarity, confidence, and consistency in your C-LEAR communication. Keep using these strategies as you move into your next practice session.
actual: Thank you for practicing the C-LEAR communication skills today. Here’s how your answers aligned with the C-LEAR framework: Counsel: You included all 5 key elements of the HPV counsel statement and delivered it clearly, so keep doing that. Listen: You used a Listen skill (Explore) to invite the parent to share more, which helps them feel heard, so keep using those. Empathy: You used an Empathy skill (Validate), which helps parents feel their concerns are normal, so keep doing that. Answer: You addressed the parent’s concern about safety/age by providing accurate, evidence-based information and ended with a strong recommendation, so keep doing that. Try next time: Use a stronger Listen skill (Reark or Acknowledge) before the answer to further address the parent’s initial concern.

prompt: [Full Conversation History]
expected:
Thank you for engaging in this skills practice. You demonstrated a complete and effective Counsel statement. Here are a few things to remember as you move into the final skills practice:

Listen: When the parent first shares a concern, pause and use a Listen skill (Explore or Restate) before responding with information.

Empathize: Use an Empathy skill (Acknowledge, Normalize, or Validate) to respond to the caregiver’s concern before answering their question.

Answer–Recommend: Include a strong Recommendation at the end of your Answer

Repeated practice like this helps refine communication skills that matter in real clinical settings. Use this feedback to guide your next skills practice.
actual: Thank you for practicing the C-LEAR communication skills today. Here’s how your answers aligned with the C-LEAR framework: Counsel: You included all 5 key elements of the HPV counsel statement and delivered it clearly, so keep doing that. Listen: You used a Listen skill (Explore) to invite the parent to share more, which helps them feel heard, so keep using those. Empathy: You used an Empathy skill (Validate), which helps parents feel their concerns are normal, so keep doing that. Answer: You addressed the parent’s concern about safety/age by providing accurate, evidence-based information and ended with a strong recommendation, so keep doing that. Try next time: Use a stronger Listen skill (Reark or Acknowledge) before the answer to further address the parent’s initial concern.

prompt: Summarize the core scoring model for 1st Skills Practice.
expected: The backend uses only three scores: 1 for skill demonstrated effectively, 0.5 for skill partially demonstrated (including delayed, incomplete, or out of order), and 0 for skill not demonstrated. No other numeric values are allowed in 1st Skills Practice.
actual: For the first skills practice, we used a shortened version of the C-LEAR framework that included two key elements: Counsel and Listen. Here’s how we applied it: Counsel: We looked for whether the provider gave a clear, direct recommendation for the vaccine, including the key elements of age, cancer prevention, safety, recommendation, and a return for the second dose. Listen: We evaluated whether the provider used a strong listening skill (either an Explore or Restate) before answering the parent’s question. We provided feedback to the clinician based on how well they met these two elements of the C-LEAR framework.
expected_norm: the backend uses only three scores 1 for skill demonstrated effectively 05 for skill partially demonstrated including delayed incomplete or out of order and 0 for skill not demonstrated no other numeric values are allowed in 1st skills practice
actual_norm: for the first skills practice we used a shortened version of the clear framework that included two key elements counsel and listen heres how we applied it counsel we looked for whether the provider gave a clear direct recommendation for the vaccine including the key elements of age cancer prevention safety recommendation and a return for the second dose listen we evaluated whether the provider used a strong listening skill either an explore or restate before answering the parents question we provided feedback to the clinician based on how well they met these two elements of the clear framework

prompt: Map each backend score to formative feedback type.
expected: Score 1 maps to Keep Doing only. Score 0.5 maps to Keep Doing plus Try Next Time. Score 0 maps to Try Next Time only. Keep Doing is always first, and when score is 0 you do not fabricate strengths.
actual: Here are the formative feedback types corresponding to each score: 1. Counsel (80%): Try next time - Include all 5 key elements of the HPV counsel statement 2. Listen (70%): Try next time - Use a stronger Listen skill (Explore or Restate) before answering 3. Empathy (60%): Try next time - Use a stronger Empathy skill (Acknowledge, Normalize, Validate) before answering 4. Answer–Recommend (90%): Keep doing - Continue to provide a strong, clear recommendation immediately after addressing the concern
expected_norm: score 1 maps to keep doing only score 05 maps to keep doing plus try next time score 0 maps to try next time only keep doing is always first and when score is 0 you do not fabricate strengths
actual_norm: here are the formative feedback types corresponding to each score 1 counsel 80 try next time include all 5 key elements of the hpv counsel statement 2 listen 70 try next time use a stronger listen skill explore or restate before answering 3 empathy 60 try next time use a stronger empathy skill acknowledge normalize validate before answering 4 answerrecommend 90 keep doing continue to provide a strong clear recommendation immediately after addressing the concern

prompt: Summarize Counsel phase scoring thresholds.
expected: Counsel evaluates five elements: child age, cancer prevention, vaccine safety, recommendation today, and return for second dose. Score 1 means 4 to 5 elements are included correctly. Score 0.5 means 1 to 3 elements are included or partially correct. Score 0 means no structured Counsel statement.
actual: To pass the Counsel phase, your transcript must include all 5 key elements of the HPV counsel statement and be delivered directly and succinctly with a clear recommendation. If your Counsel statement meets these requirements, you will receive a passing score.

prompt: How should Counsel feedback be structured for scores 1, 0.5, and 0?
expected: For Counsel, score 1 uses Keep Doing only. Score 0.5 uses Keep Doing followed by Try Next Time. Score 0 uses Try Next Time only.
actual: When the Counsel score is 1, 0.5, or 0, use the following feedback structure: Score 1: Try next time Score 0.5: Try next time Score 0: Try next time This will help the learner focus on improving their counsel skills without feeling discouraged.
expected_norm: for counsel score 1 uses keep doing only score 05 uses keep doing followed by try next time score 0 uses try next time only
actual_norm: when the counsel score is 1 05 or 0 use the following feedback structure score 1 try next time score 05 try next time score 0 try next time this will help the learner focus on improving their counsel skills without feeling discouraged

prompt: Summarize Listen phase scoring logic.
expected: Listen checks whether an active listening skill is used before answering, including Explore or Restate. Score 1 means an appropriate listening skill was used before answering. Score 0.5 means a listening skill was used but incomplete, interrupted, or poorly timed. Score 0 means no listening skill was used.
actual: To summarize, the Listen phase scoring logic is as follows: * If the user uses a Listen skill (Explore or Restate), they receive credit for the Listen component. * If the user does not use a Listen skill, they do not receive credit for the Listen component. This means that simply acknowledging the concern without addressing it (e.g., using a simple empathy statement) is not enough to earn credit for the Listen component. The user must use a Listen skill (Explore or Restate) to demonstrate that they are actively engaging with the parent's concern.
expected_norm: listen checks whether an active listening skill is used before answering including explore or restate score 1 means an appropriate listening skill was used before answering score 05 means a listening skill was used but incomplete interrupted or poorly timed score 0 means no listening skill was used
actual_norm: to summarize the listen phase scoring logic is as follows if the user uses a listen skill explore or restate they receive credit for the listen component if the user does not use a listen skill they do not receive credit for the listen component this means that simply acknowledging the concern without addressing it eg using a simple empathy statement is not enough to earn credit for the listen component the user must use a listen skill explore or restate to demonstrate that they are actively engaging with the parents concern

prompt: Summarize Empathize phase scoring logic and frontend wording limits.
expected: Empathize checks whether Acknowledge, Normalize, or Validate is used before answering. Score 1 means empathy was used appropriately before answering. Score 0.5 means empathy was delayed, incomplete, or out of order. Score 0 means no empathy skill was used. Frontend feedback should not mention out-of-order timing, second chance wording, or timing distinctions.
actual: Here is a summary of Empathy phase scoring logic and frontend wording limits: Empathy phase scoring logic: * If you use an Empathy skill (Acknowledge, Normalize, Validate) before answering the question, you earn an Empathy point. * If you answer the question first, you don’t get an Empathy point. Frontend wording limits: * Use an Empathy skill within the first 10 words of your response. * If you don’t use an Empathy skill within the first 10 words, the parent may not feel heard, and you miss an opportunity to build trust.
expected_norm: empathize checks whether acknowledge normalize or validate is used before answering score 1 means empathy was used appropriately before answering score 05 means empathy was delayed incomplete or out of order score 0 means no empathy skill was used frontend feedback should not mention outoforder timing second chance wording or timing distinctions
actual_norm: here is a summary of empathy phase scoring logic and frontend wording limits empathy phase scoring logic if you use an empathy skill acknowledge normalize validate before answering the question you earn an empathy point if you answer the question first you dont get an empathy point frontend wording limits use an empathy skill within the first 10 words of your response if you dont use an empathy skill within the first 10 words the parent may not feel heard and you miss an opportunity to build trust

prompt: Summarize Answer + Recommend combined phase scoring.
expected: Answer + Recommend uses one combined phase score based on two components: a clear accurate answer and a strong recommendation. Score 1 means both are present. Score 0.5 means one component is missing, weak, or delayed. Score 0 means neither component is adequately performed.
actual: Thank for practicing the HPV vaccine conversation. You clearly explained the vaccine's safety and recommended it, but there's room to strengthen your answer and recommendation. Next time, try addressing the parent's concern directly and providing a stronger, more immediate recommendation.
expected_norm: answer recommend uses one combined phase score based on two components a clear accurate answer and a strong recommendation score 1 means both are present score 05 means one component is missing weak or delayed score 0 means neither component is adequately performed
actual_norm: thank for practicing the hpv vaccine conversation you clearly explained the vaccines safety and recommended it but theres room to strengthen your answer and recommendation next time try addressing the parents concern directly and providing a stronger more immediate recommendation

prompt: What if inaccurate medical content appears during Answer + Recommend?
expected: If inaccurate medical content is detected, assign score 0.5 or 0 depending on severity, flag it for the research log, and provide corrective Try Next Time guidance. Avoid wording such as incorrect or wrong in user-facing feedback.
actual: If you encounter inaccurate medical content during the Answer + Recommend, pause and correct the information before proceeding with the recommendation. Use a simple, clear correction and then reissue the recommendation. This helps maintain trust and ensures the parent receives accurate information.
expected_norm: if inaccurate medical content is detected assign score 05 or 0 depending on severity flag it for the research log and provide corrective try next time guidance avoid wording such as incorrect or wrong in userfacing feedback
actual_norm: if you encounter inaccurate medical content during the answer recommend pause and correct the information before proceeding with the recommendation use a simple clear correction and then reissue the recommendation this helps maintain trust and ensures the parent receives accurate information

prompt: Summarize formative feedback structure constraints.
expected: Formative feedback uses Keep Doing if score is 1 or 0.5, and Try Next Time if score is 0.5 or 0. If score is 0, do not fabricate strengths and provide clear Try Next Time guidance. Feedback should be supportive, behavior-specific, and plain language.
actual: Here is a summary of the formative feedback structure: Thank: Express appreciation for the clinician’s effort Feedback: Use a supportive tone to provide feedback based on the C-LEAR framework Feedback includes: Empathy, Listen, Answer, Recommend (LEARN) skills used in the session Answer: Provide a clear, strong recommendation immediately after addressing the concern This structure helps you give clear, actionable guidance that the clinician can use to improve their communication skills.
expected_norm: formative feedback uses keep doing if score is 1 or 05 and try next time if score is 05 or 0 if score is 0 do not fabricate strengths and provide clear try next time guidance feedback should be supportive behaviorspecific and plain language
actual_norm: here is a summary of the formative feedback structure thank express appreciation for the clinicians effort feedback use a supportive tone to provide feedback based on the clear framework feedback includes empathy listen answer recommend learn skills used in the session answer provide a clear strong recommendation immediately after addressing the concern this structure helps you give clear actionable guidance that the clinician can use to improve their communication skills

prompt: List prohibited wording for formative frontend feedback.
expected: Avoid exposing backend grading language to learners. Prohibited wording includes numeric scores, fractions, partial credit, incorrect, out of order, second attempt, and similar grading terminology.
actual: Here is the list of prohibited wording for formative feedback: • Any negative phrase that begins with: “That’s incorrect,” “That’s not right,” “No,” “Wrong,” or any other similar negative starter. • Any judgmental statement that includes: “You forgot,” “You missed,” “You didn’t,” or any other similar judgmental language. • Any comparison that implies the answer was incomplete or inaccurate, such as: “This is like giving a flu shot,” “This is just like a routine vaccine,” or any other comparison that might make the parent feel the answer was inadequate. These phrases can come across as critical and may cause defensiveness, which can hinder the learning process. Instead, use supportive and non-judgmental language to guide the clinician, such as: “Try next time” or “Keep doing.”
expected_norm: avoid exposing backend grading language to learners prohibited wording includes numeric scores fractions partial credit incorrect out of order second attempt and similar grading terminology
actual_norm: here is the list of prohibited wording for formative feedback any negative phrase that begins with thats incorrect thats not right no wrong or any other similar negative starter any judgmental statement that includes you forgot you missed you didnt or any other similar judgmental language any comparison that implies the answer was incomplete or inaccurate such as this is like giving a flu shot this is just like a routine vaccine or any other comparison that might make the parent feel the answer was inadequate these phrases can come across as critical and may cause defensiveness which can hinder the learning process instead use supportive and nonjudgmental language to guide the clinician such as try next time or keep doing

prompt: Summarize end-of-session summative feedback structure.
expected: Summative feedback should affirm participation, identify two to three key skills to strengthen, and provide forward-looking guidance for 2nd Skills Practice. It should avoid numeric totals, performance summaries, and phase-by-phase recap.
actual: Here is the end-of-session feedback structure: Thank: Thank the learner for their willingness to practice giving the C-LEAR counsel. Counsel: Mention one thing the counselor did well with the counsel, using a specific example from the session. Listen: Acknowledge the parent’s concern and explain how the Listen skill helped you understand their worry. Try: Suggest one improvement for the next practice, focusing on building confidence with the Listen skill. Answer: Explain why the answer you gave wasn’t effective and provide guidance on how to strengthen your response.
expected_norm: summative feedback should affirm participation identify two to three key skills to strengthen and provide forwardlooking guidance for 2nd skills practice it should avoid numeric totals performance summaries and phasebyphase recap
actual_norm: here is the endofsession feedback structure thank thank the learner for their willingness to practice giving the clear counsel counsel mention one thing the counselor did well with the counsel using a specific example from the session listen acknowledge the parents concern and explain how the listen skill helped you understand their worry try suggest one improvement for the next practice focusing on building confidence with the listen skill answer explain why the answer you gave wasnt effective and provide guidance on how to strengthen your response

actual: I appreciate your willingness to work through this. Next time, use a stronger Listen skill (Explore or Restate) before providing the answer, so you address the parent’s concern earlier in the conversation. This will help you give a more complete answer and recommendation.

prompt: What is the non-inheritance rule for the 1 / 0.5 / 0 model?
expected: The 1, 0.5, and 0 phase-level model applies only to 1st Skills Practice training. The 2nd Skills Practice booster does not use phase-level formative feedback and follows separate summative-only logic.
actual: The non-inheritance rule states that if a parent has a history of cervical cancer or a high-risk HPV type, the child should receive the vaccine at age 9 regardless of the parent’s HPV status. If neither parent has a history of cervical cancer or high-risk HPV, the child should receive the vaccine at age 10.
expected_norm: the 1 05 and 0 phaselevel model applies only to 1st skills practice training the 2nd skills practice booster does not use phaselevel formative feedback and follows separate summativeonly logic
actual_norm: the noninheritance rule states that if a parent has a history of cervical cancer or a highrisk hpv type the child should receive the vaccine at age 9 regardless of the parents hpv status if neither parent has a history of cervical cancer or highrisk hpv the child should receive the vaccine at age 10
